name: Familial-longevity cohorts had lower autosomal mCA burden than controls
summary: Centenarian and familial-longevity data connect lower mosaic chromosomal alteration burden with exceptional human aging phenotypes.
statement: Centenarians and offspring accumulated fewer autosomal mosaic chromosomal alterations than controls, suggesting that limited mCA accumulation may be associated with extreme human longevity.
population_or_model: NECS and LLFS centenarians, offspring, familial-longevity subjects, and controls
time_horizon: Cohort and meta-analysis across longevity studies
quantitative_note: Centenarians and offspring had lower autosomal mCA risk than controls (relative risk 0.637, P=0.0147); mCAs were associated with cancer and dementia hazards at 10% FDR.
caveats: The finding is observational and cannot prove that preventing mCAs extends lifespan.
The mCA prevalence plateau above age 102 could reflect survivor selection.
The measured compartment is blood-derived DNA.
